result:
I.  
The first light is always blue. Not the sentimental blue of postcards or the cinematic blue that signals “night” on a suburban screen, but the bruised, almost ultraviolet hush that leaks in before the sun decides to commit. I’ve watched it crawl across a hundred ceilings now—plaster, stucco, corrugated tin, painted-over-water-damage—and it behaves like a liquid more than a color, pooling in the corners first, then climbing down the walls as though looking for an exit it will never find. By the time the light turns gold I’ve usually forgotten what I was thinking about in the dark, which is probably merciful. The mind at four-thirty a.m. is a dog that has slipped its collar: it dashes into neighbors’ gardens, rolls in unspeakable smells, returns proud and filthy while you stand on the porch in your bathrobe, pretending you never saw the hole in the fence.

II.  
Memory is not a card catalog; it is a bucket of rusted nails you keep tripping over in the garage. You swear you’ll label it, move it, throw it out, but every spring there it is, and one nail always finds the tender arch of your foot. The first time I understood this I was eight, sitting on the carpet of my grandmother’s den, tracing the pattern of an afghan whose colors smelled like her perfume—White Shoulders, cigarettes, and the sweet rot of paperback novels. I can still reproduce that braid in my mind, orange into green into brown, but I cannot tell you what my grandmother’s face looked like unless I conjure a photograph. The brain keeps the texture, jettisons the image, as if to say: you don’t need her cheekbones, you need the way the wool scratched the inside of your wrist when she tucked you in. That is how she loved you; the rest was scaffolding.

III.  
There is a small museum in southern Iceland devoted to things found in the stomachs of cod. I arrived there by accident, hitchhiking on the ring road with a woman who believed elves controlled the traffic lights. She let me out at a gravel patch barely wide enough for two Volkswagens and pointed toward a corrugated-metal shed the color of old snow. Inside, jars floated on shelves: buttons, doll arms, cigarette filters, a tiny silver spoon monogrammed E.M.M. A curator with a beard like iced moss told me each object had been donated by a fisherman who’d slit open a catch and discovered something that made him reluctant to fry. I asked if anyone had ever claimed an item. He laughed, a sound like glaciers cracking, and said, “People don’t want back what the sea has half digested.” I stared at the spoon until the letters rearranged themselves into M.E. Whoever E.M.M. was, she had once stirred sugar into coffee or fed a child medicine, and now her initial was inverted, suspended in brine, a ghost of order inside a fish that had probably ended up as fish sticks. I thought about the afghan, about the nails, about the way we mislay entire lives and still manage to go on feeding ourselves.

IV.  
Freud’s grandson invented the fort-da game: fling the spool behind the crib skirt, reel it back with a string, cry “gone,” cry “there.” Supposedly this teaches the infant that absence is temporary, that mother returns. What nobody mentions is that the spool is always scarred by the third act: teeth marks, chipped paint, a crack spiraling outward like the fault line of a future earthquake. We practice loss on objects so that when it finally happens to people we have the muscle memory. Still, the body keeps score in its own dumb ledger—tight hip flexor the week of the breakup, acid reflux on the anniversary of the funeral. The mind says, “Processed and archived,” while the calf muscle at 2:13 a.m. whispers, “Liar.”

V.  
When I was twenty-five I lived above a noodle shop that stayed open until the sky went bruise-blue again. The owner, Mrs. Kim, kept a stack of student IDs she had confiscated for fake birthdates. She rubber-banded them like a deck of forbidden playing cards and shuffled through them when business slowed. “I remember every liar,” she told me once, tapping a card whose photo showed me with platinum hair and a T-shirt that said FREE WINONA. “You were nicer then,” she said, though I couldn’t tell if she meant the me in the picture or the me standing in front of her holding a Styrofoam bowl of soup. After she died—stroke, mid-rush, a ladle clattering into the stockpot like a bell no one meant to ring—her son found the rubber band broken, IDs scattered across the office floor. He threw them all away. I keep thinking one is still wedged behind the prep counter, warping in the steam, my younger face dissolving into lamination glue. Maybe in a decade a repairman will pull the range out to service the gas line and find me there, smiling like someone who believed inconvenience was the worst life could dish out.

VI.  
I used to write poems on the bus, literal back-of-transfer poems, because the motion made me nauseous if I looked up. One line per transfer, then fold and stuff into the back pocket of whatever novel I was pretending to read. Eventually the pockets grew heavy, paper cuts blooming along my fingertips like paperwhites. I told myself that when I had enough I’d arrange them into something vast and honest, a cardboard cathedral of small griefs. Instead, one May afternoon, I did laundry without checking the jeans. The agitation transformed two years of evidence into lint the color of cigarette ash. I stood in the laundromat holding a damp brick of congealed words and felt nothing except the spin cycle continuing inside my chest. Later that week I tried to reconstruct a single stanza from memory. All I could recover was a half-sentence that ended with the word “salt.” I no longer knew whether it referred to tears, sweat, seasoning, or the oceanic smudge of freeway air. The failure felt like justice: language returning to pulp, punctuation sinking like fishing weights. Since then I travel with a phone full of cloud drafts, everything synced, backed up, encrypted—yet I still lose more phrases than I save. The digital erasure leaves no lint, only an almost-erotic lightness, as if a pickpocket has lifted the hour from my coat and left the coat cleaner.

VII.  
The etymology of “husband” is Old Norse: húsbóndi, “master of the house.” The etymology of “wife” is braided from several roots, one of which means “veiled.” I didn’t know this when I married in a courthouse where the air-conditioning sobbed against the summer. We said the words, signed the tablet, kissed while a stranger in cargo shorts took a cellphone video in portrait mode. Outside, heat spiraled off the asphalt like transparent ivy. That night we ate drive-through tacos and watched a documentary about people who free-climb cell towers for fun. One silhouetted figure said the trick is to never look down long enough to recognize your own street. I thought that sounded like marriage, like love, like most of the commitments we make: climb, clip in, don’t catalogue the drop. Four years later we separated on the same concrete steps where we’d posed for selfies, only now the banister was tagged with a neon-green penis and someone had dropped a neon-pink party balloon caught on the railing, sagging like an exhausted lung. There was no argument left to have, just two people holding reusable grocery bags that contained the precise amount of stuff one can carry on a city bus. I kept the phone charger; she took the vegetable peeler. Neither of us wanted the waffle iron because it had become a symbol of Sunday mornings we no longer wished to interpret. I walked the long way home past the noodle shop, which had turned into a bubble-tea franchise. The neon waves of digital koi swam across the window where Mrs. Kim’s handwritten menu used to hang. I felt the spin cycle again, slower now, a lullaby for things that refuse to come clean.

VIII.  
Here is a partial list of places I have cried:  
- The last row of an Icelandic whale-watching vessel, mist salted into my eyelashes.  
- A Tokyo hotel corridor at 5:00 a.m., because the vending machine ate my coins and I realized no one in the world knew my room number.  
- The produce aisle of a grocery store in Portland, facing a pyramid of figs that reminded me of a medication my grandfather took—brown, soft, split at the seam.  
- A rental car outside Amarillo, soundtracked by a podcast about the Donner Party, the key turned to auxiliary so the AC stayed on.  
- The shower of an Airbnb shaped like a geodesic dome, the water pooling around my feet because the drain was clogged with previous guests’ hair—blonde, brunette, red, a tiny mammal of strangers’ sorrow.  
Each episode felt terminal, yet I can barely recall the contours of the grief. Only the settings remain, like stage backdrops struck after the play closes, waiting for the next troupe to claim them.

IX.  
My father keeps a box of obsolete chargers in his basement, labeled “Junk” in Sharpie on flaking masking tape. Whenever I visit he asks if I need one, hopeful, as though the abandoned cords might re-home themselves. I always say no, but I secretly love the box. It is the most candid autobiography he will ever compose: the fat circular plug from the first clamshell phone, the serrated cylinder of a camcorder, the miniature USB like a blackened incisor. Together they chart a map of family vacations, birthdays, late-night calls announcing deaths and births. The electricity has drained out of every one, yet they retain the shape of anticipation. Sometimes I imagine the afterlife looking like that: a dim corridor where you are offered every cord you ever misplaced, and you must stand there attempting to match plug to port for eternity. Every so often you find a fit, the satisfying click of metal entering metal, and for a moment the bulb flickers on—then the voltage proves too much, the filament pops, darkness reasserts itself. Still, you move to the next cable, because hope is a choreography even the dead can’t resign.

X.  
In graduate school I had a seminar on “the ethics of witnessing.” We read Levinas, Butler, memoirs of atrocity until the words aligned like freight cars in a dream you can’t uncouple. One afternoon the professor dimmed the lights and showed us a video shot by a humanitarian drone flying over a refugee camp. The thermal camera rendered bodies as white commas against indigo tents. A child darted into the alley between shelters and the software auto-tracked him, painting a red rectangle around his heat signature. The rectangle looked like a coffin made of light. After the screening we sat in silence so total I could hear the fluorescent bulb stutter. Finally a woman in the back said, “I feel obscene.” She didn’t elaborate, and nobody asked her to. The next week she dropped the course. I stayed, wrote a paper arguing that saturation itself was a form of violence, that there must exist a threshold past which empathy collapses into aesthetic reflex. The professor gave me an A minus and wrote in the margin, “But where is that threshold? And who gets to set it?” I never answered, not in the footnotes, not in the dreams that followed, not in the decade since. Sometimes the drone footage reappears behind my eyelids when I’m trying to fall asleep, the red rectangle sliding across the screen like a cursor that cannot choose, cannot close the tab.

XI.  
I want to tell you something beautiful now, because even in a free write there is etiquette: emotional arc, redemption, lift of wing at the end. Here is the best I can do.  
One December I took a night train from Budapest to Bucharest. The carriage smelled of metal and wet wool. Outside, snow erased the border between field and sky, so the windows became mirrors flecked with black telephone poles that stuttered past like film reel. A man in the compartment across from me unwrapped a paper package and offered me goose liver; I produced oranges; we shared without language. At 2:13 a.m. the train stopped in the middle of nothing, engines cut, lights dimmed. Conductors strode the corridor speaking Hungarian, then Romanian, then something that might have been Ukrainian. Passengers pressed faces to glass, but there was only our own reflection and the dark. After twenty minutes a whisper moved through the car: we had hit something—elk, dog, maybe a person. The protocols required we wait for the border police, forms, explanation. People groaned, checked watches, lit cigarettes though it was forbidden. The man and I sat in our mutual silence. He peeled an orange and the citrus oil flared like a match. I had the sudden conviction that whatever lay on the tracks outside was already part of the snow now, that in the morning children would sled over it unknowing. And this thought did not horrify me; it felt like mercy, the landscape’s way of absolving us. When the train finally lurched forward, no official came to explain. We rolled on as if the halt had been a collective dream. At daybreak Bucharest appeared, low and concrete, stray dogs trotting along the rails. I stepped onto the platform changed in a way I still cannot name, carrying only the smell of orange on my fingers and the certainty that forgiveness can arrive without a priest, without a verdict, without even an apology—just the dark agreeing to close over what cannot be repaired.

XII.  
I used to think the opposite of forgetting was remembering. Now I believe the opposite of forgetting is assembling. We do not retrieve the past; we collage it. The brain snips here, glues there, crops the ex-lover’s elbow from the beach photo and replaces it with a seagull that might never have flown that day. Each time we speak the story we glue another scrap on top. Eventually the original image is buried beneath strata of later versions, a palimpsest thick as sidewalk ice in March. This is why two siblings can “remember” the same parent so differently: they are describing two separate mosaics that happen to share a few tiles. Understanding this has made me gentler at dinner parties when someone corrects my anecdote—“No, it was raining, not snowing,”—because I see the panic in their eyes: if the weather changes, the whole cathedral of self wobbles. I let them have their rain. I keep my snow to myself, flakes drifting across a yard that may never have existed, falling nevertheless on the tongue of a child who still believes winter is a form of protection, a soft eraser that makes the world quiet enough to hear your own heart.

XIII.  
The heart, by the way, sounds nothing like the ocean. Stethoscopes reveal more of a muffled gunfight—valves snapping shut, blood accelerating around curves, the occasional gurgle of a turbulent stream. I recorded mine once at one of those science-center kiosks that promise to convert your heartbeat into a percussive loop. I stood with my finger in the plethysmograph and watched the green waveform skitter across the screen. The software layered drums under it, rendered it in 4/4 time, offered to email the MP3. Listening later through headphones, I recognized nothing of myself in the slick club beat. It sounded like music made by someone who had never drowned, never fallen in love, never stood in a grocery aisle paralyzed by figs. I deleted the file, but it reappears sometimes in the shuffle of communal playlists, piped into spin classes where strangers sweat and grunt in fluorescent rooms. I imagine their cells vibrating to my arrhythmia, an accidental communion, my past becoming their uphill climb.

XIV.  
Here is what I have learned about uphill: the metaphor is dishonest. Progress is less a slope than a series of false flats where the muscles relax just long enough to be blindsided by the next grade. You think you’ve arrived because the burning stops, but the road only levels to gather its breath before it lunges. People therefore put too much faith in the concept “plateau,” imagining a mesa where struggle picnics under junipers. In reality the plateau is the place you stop noticing the altitude, which is why altitude sickness kills more hikers on the way down than up. The lesson applies to grief, to graduate seminars, to marriage, to writing two-thousand-five-hundred words without a compass: acclimatize secretly, keep sipping water, never trust the moment it feels easy.

XV.  
I check the word count and discover I have exceeded my allowance yet still not mentioned the spider who lived in the driver’s-side mirror of my car for an entire summer. I watched her rebuild the web every morning as I drove to teach, strands silvering in the slipstream, insects pancaking against the glass. Each night the wind of the commute shredded her architecture. Each dawn she started over, abdomen pulsing like a tiny black heart. One Friday I found the web intact and her body gone, legs curled into a comma on the windowsill. I drove the whole route crying behind sunglasses, though I could not name the bereavement. Was I mourning the spider? The redundancy of labor? The certainty that I too was spinning something hourly disintegrated by speed? I left the web alone until the next rain, when it collapsed into water threads and blew away. The mirror felt suddenly naked, reflecting only my eye bags, the asymmetrical slump of my shoulders. I started parking farther from the building after that, hoping another arachnid might colonize the space, but none arrived. Absence is also an architecture, just harder to photograph.

XVI.  
We are nearing the mandated border, though borders are fictions, ask any glacier. I could trail off midsentence like a person walking into fog, or I could manufacture a crescendo—brass section, sunrise, reconciliation of warring selves. Instead I will offer the only wisdom I own that costs nothing to repeat: pay attention to what you do with your hands when you are supposedly doing nothing. They will reveal the sub Rosa narrative: thumb worrying the label of a beer bottle, index finger tapping the pulse at the throat, nail picking dried wax off the tablecloth. The hands are like auxiliary hearts, keeping peripheral beat, storing in their calluses every unspoken vow. If you can bring yourself to watch them without shame, you will locate the next stitch in the tapestry, the next knot in the net you’re secretly weaving to hold whatever is poised to fall. The trick is not to arrest the fall but to predict its trajectory, to position the net in the dark, to trust that something—memory, hunger, the blue light before dawn—will drop into it, and you will feel the small impact, the corded tug of having caught yourself still here, still here.

I imagine their cells vibrating to my arrhythmia, an accidental communion, my past becoming their uphill climb.

XIV.  
Here is what I have learned about uphill: the metaphor is dishonest. Progress is less a slope than a series of false flats where the muscles relax just long enough to be blindsided by the next grade. You think you’ve arrived because the burning stops, but the road only levels to gather its breath before it lunges. People therefore put too much faith in the concept “plateau,” imagining a mesa where struggle picnics under junipers. In reality the plateau is the place you stop noticing the altitude, which is why altitude sickness kills more hikers on the way down than up. The lesson applies to grief, to graduate seminars, to marriage, to writing two-thousand-five-hundred words without a compass: acclimatize secretly, keep sipping water, never trust the moment it feels easy.

XV.  
I check the word count and discover I have exceeded my allowance yet still not mentioned the spider who lived in the driver’s-side mirror of my car for an entire summer. I watched her rebuild the web every morning as I drove to teach, strands silvering in the slipstream, insects pancaking against the glass. Each night the wind of the commute shredded her architecture. Each dawn she started over, abdomen pulsing like a tiny black heart. One Friday I found the web intact and her body gone, legs curled into a comma on the windowsill. I drove the whole route crying behind sunglasses, though I could not name the bereavement. Was I mourning the spider? The redundancy of labor? The certainty that I too was spinning something hourly disintegrated by speed? I left the web alone until the next rain, when it collapsed into water threads and blew away. The mirror felt suddenly naked, reflecting only my eye bags, the asymmetrical slump of my shoulders. I started parking farther from the building after that, hoping another arachnid might colonize the space, but none arrived. Absence is also an architecture, just harder to photograph.

XVI.  
We are nearing the mandated border, though borders are fictions, ask any glacier. I could trail off midsentence like a person walking into fog, or I could manufacture a crescendo—brass section, sunrise, reconciliation of warring selves. Instead I will offer the only wisdom I own that costs nothing to repeat: pay attention to what you do with your hands when you are supposedly doing nothing. They will reveal the sub Rosa narrative: thumb worrying the label of a beer bottle, index finger tapping the pulse at the throat, nail picking dried wax off the tablecloth. The hands are like auxiliary hearts, keeping peripheral beat, storing in their calluses every unspoken vow. If you can bring yourself to watch them without shame, you will locate the next stitch in the tapestry, the next knot in the net you’re secretly weaving to hold whatever is poised to fall. The trick is not to arrest the fall but to predict its trajectory, to position the net in the dark, to trust that something—memory, hunger, the blue light before dawn—will drop into it, and you will feel the small impact, the corded tug of having caught yourself still here, still here.

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 2500 words.
